meta.disclaimer: This protocol is for informational purposes only. It does not replace professional medical advice. When in doubt, always seek emergency medical care.

assessSeverity.description: Ask yourself these questions to gauge flare severity on a scale of mild, moderate, or severe.

questions.question: How many bowel movements are you having per day?
questions.severe: More than 10 per day, entirely liquid or incontinent

questions.question: Is there blood in your stool?
questions.severe: Large amounts of blood, passing clots, or blood without stool

questions.question: What is your pain level (0-10)?
questions.severe: 7-10, constant severe pain, unable to function

questions.question: Are you able to eat and drink?
questions.mild: Eating reduced amounts but keeping food down
questions.severe: Unable to eat or drink, vomiting everything

questions.question: Do you have a fever?
questions.severe: High fever (>100.4F / >38C) or chills and rigors

questions.question: How is your energy level?
questions.mild: Somewhat fatigued but can do most activities
questions.moderate: Very fatigued, difficulty completing daily tasks
questions.severe: Unable to get out of bed, dizziness on standing

questions.question: Are you showing signs of dehydration?
questions.moderate: Dry mouth, reduced urine output, darker urine
questions.severe: Very dry mouth, little or no urine, rapid heart rate, dizziness

questions.question: Do you have any abdominal distension or rigidity?
questions.severe: Rigid abdomen, rebound tenderness, unable to tolerate touch

immediateActions.description: Step-by-step actions to take when a flare begins.

steps.details: Use the severity assessment questions above. Log your symptoms including bowel movement count, blood amount, pain level, temperature, and time of onset.

Start sipping clear fluids: oral rehydration solution (ORS), diluted electrolyte drinks, clear broth, or water with a pinch of salt and sugar. Aim for at least 8 oz every hour. Avoid sugary drinks and caffeine.
Stop all raw fruits, vegetables, whole grains, seeds, and nuts. Switch to white rice, plain chicken, bananas, applesauce, toast (BRAT), bone broth, and well-cooked peeled potatoes.

Ensure you have not missed any doses. Do NOT increase steroid dose without doctor approval. If you have a rescue medication prescribed (e.g., budesonide, prednisone taper), check instructions for when to start it.
Call during business hours. Most GI offices have same-day nurse advice lines. Describe your symptoms clearly and note duration. Ask about adjusting medications.

Use a heating pad on the abdomen. Take acetaminophen (Tylenol) if needed. Do NOT take NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen, aspirin) as they can worsen Crohn's inflammation. Try warm baths for perianal discomfort.
Cancel non-essential commitments. Stress can amplify flare symptoms. Practice deep breathing or guided meditation. Sleep is essential for recovery.
Record bowel movements (time, consistency, blood), food intake, fluid intake, pain level, temperature, and medications taken. This log is invaluable for your doctor.

whenToCallDoctor.description: Contact your gastroenterologist's office (same day or next business day) if you experience any of the following.
criteria: More than 6 loose/bloody stools per day for more than 2 days
Persistent abdominal pain not relieved by your usual measures
Low-grade fever (99-100.4F / 37.2-38C) lasting more than 24 hours
Blood in stool that is new or significantly increased
Unable to keep down medications for more than 24 hours
Worsening symptoms despite current treatment plan
New joint pain, eye redness or pain, or skin lesions (extra-intestinal manifestations)
Unintentional weight loss of more than 5 pounds in a week
New or worsening perianal pain, drainage, or swelling
Symptoms that have not improved after 3-5 days of prescribed rescue therapy
Any concern about your symptoms or medication side effects
Need for refills on rescue medications (e.g., prednisone)

whenToGoER.description: Go to the emergency room immediately or call 911 if you experience any of the following.
criteria: Severe abdominal pain that is constant and worsening, especially if abdomen is rigid or distended
Passing large amounts of blood - soaking through pads, passing large clots, or blood without stool
Signs of significant dehydration: dizziness or fainting on standing, rapid heart rate, confusion, no urination for 8+ hours
High fever (>101.3F / >38.5C) especially if on immunosuppressive medications
Persistent vomiting - unable to keep down any fluids for more than 12 hours
Signs of bowel obstruction: complete inability to pass gas or stool, severe bloating, bilious (green) vomiting
Signs of perforation: sudden severe pain that spreads across the abdomen, rigid abdomen, rebound tenderness
Signs of abscess: localized severe pain with high fever, chills, and rigors
Chest pain, shortness of breath, or calf swelling (blood clot risk is elevated in IBD flares)
Severe perianal pain with fever suggesting perianal abscess
Confusion, altered consciousness, or inability to stay awake
Any symptom that makes you feel your life is in danger - trust your instincts

whatToTellER.description: Information to communicate quickly and clearly to ER staff. Prepare this in advance.

checklist.detail: State: 'I have Crohn's disease' - specify location (ileal, colonic, ileocolonic) and pattern (inflammatory, stricturing, penetrating) if you know it.
List ALL medications including biologics (name, dose, last injection/infusion date), immunomodulators, steroids, and supplements. Bring medication list or bottles.
List all drug allergies and type of reaction (rash, anaphylaxis, etc.).
List any previous bowel surgeries (resections, strictureplasties), abscess drainages, or fistula procedures with approximate dates.
When symptoms started, what has changed, number of bowel movements per day, amount of blood, pain location and severity, fever, last oral intake.
Provide your GI doctor's name, practice, and phone number. Ask ER to contact them.
Critical: Tell ER 'I am immunosuppressed' if on biologics, immunomodulators, or steroids. This changes how infections are investigated and treated.
If recent, mention last colonoscopy date, recent CT/MRI results, recent blood work. Bring copies if available.
Inform ER you have Crohn's - NSAIDs should be avoided. Acetaminophen is safe. If opioids are needed, ask about impact on bowel function.
If CT scan is ordered, mention any previous contrast reactions. Note that bowel prep may not be possible during a flare.

hospitalBag.description: Keep a pre-packed bag ready for unexpected ER visits or hospital stays.

items.detail: Printed or digital list of ALL medications with doses and schedules. Include pharmacy contact info.
Keep photocopies in the bag. Include a copy of your health insurance card (front and back).
Long charging cable and portable battery pack. Hospital stays can be long.
Loose pajama pants or shorts with elastic waist. Easy on/off for frequent bathroom trips. Non-slip socks or slippers.
Wet wipes (fragrance-free), barrier cream (zinc oxide/Calmoseptine for perianal skin), lip balm, toothbrush, toothpaste, deodorant.
Small pillow or blanket (hospitals are cold), earplugs, eye mask, headphones.
Book, tablet, downloaded shows/podcasts. Hospital wifi can be unreliable.
Once cleared to eat: plain crackers, applesauce cups, electrolyte packets. Nothing during NPO (nothing by mouth) orders.
Printed list of emergency contacts, GI doctor, primary care doctor, pharmacy, and your health care proxy/power of attorney if applicable.
Copy of recent colonoscopy report, surgical history, and recent labs if available. USB drive or printed copies.
For writing down doctor instructions, medication changes, questions, and discharge instructions.
Bring several pairs. Accidents happen during flares and there is no shame in being prepared.

dietDuringFlare.description: Dietary recommendations during an active flare to minimize symptoms while maintaining nutrition.

recommendations: Clear liquids if tolerated: broth, oral rehydration solution, weak tea, gelatin
Sip fluids frequently - small amounts every 15-30 minutes rather than large volumes
Oral rehydration solution recipe: 1 liter water + 6 teaspoons sugar + 1/2 teaspoon salt
Avoid all solid food if vomiting or if instructed by your doctor (NPO)
Avoid caffeine, alcohol, carbonated beverages, and fruit juices (osmotic diarrhea risk)
Popsicles (non-dairy) can help with hydration if nauseous

phases.phase: Transition phase (as symptoms begin to improve)
recommendations: BRAT diet: bananas, white rice, applesauce, white toast
Bone broth - provides collagen, electrolytes, and amino acids including glycine and proline
Well-cooked, peeled, seedless vegetables: carrots, potatoes, squash
Plain chicken or turkey (baked or boiled, no skin)
Eggs (scrambled or soft-boiled)
Smooth nut butters in small amounts if tolerated
Oatmeal (well-cooked, made with water or lactose-free milk)
Eat small frequent meals (5-6 per day) rather than 3 large meals
Gradually reintroduce foods one at a time over days
Add soft, well-cooked proteins: fish (salmon is anti-inflammatory), lean meats
Introduce low-FODMAP fruits: blueberries, strawberries, cantaloupe, firm banana
Add well-cooked low-fiber vegetables: zucchini, green beans, spinach
Consider lactose-free dairy or aged cheeses if previously tolerated
Reintroduce whole grains slowly: start with white rice, then oats, then quinoa
Keep a food diary to identify individual triggers during reintroduction
Continue to avoid known personal triggers, raw vegetables, seeds, popcorn, and spicy foods until fully in remission

generalPrinciples: Stay hydrated - aim for pale yellow urine as a guide
Eat small, frequent meals rather than large ones
Chew food thoroughly - digestion begins in the mouth
Avoid very hot or very cold foods (may increase cramping)
Cook vegetables well and remove skins and seeds
Avoid high-fat fried foods (difficult to digest during inflammation)
Avoid sugar alcohols (sorbitol, mannitol, xylitol) found in sugar-free products
Limit dairy if lactose intolerant - use lactose-free alternatives
Consider liquid nutrition supplements (Ensure, Boost, Kate Farms) if unable to meet caloric needs
If you have a stricture: avoid fibrous, stringy, or chunky foods that could cause obstruction
Consult a registered dietitian who specializes in IBD for personalized guidance
